sources_audited.note: The cited Census NAICS page loaded, but its fetched content did not display code 531311, the Residential Property Managers title, or the claimed managing-for-others definition. It exposed only the generic NAICS search shell and manual link, so the specific claim could not be verified from this citation during review.

The May 2023 BLS page is explicitly for broad NAICS 531000 Real Estate and reports the cited occupational mix, including property managers, office and administrative support, customer service, financial, sales, and physical occupations. The packet discloses the broader-sector bridge.

BLS supports the stated residential/commercial/industrial manager population, contracted property-management-firm model, leasing, collections, inspection, repair coordination, records, budgets, reporting, compliance duties, and 4 percent 2024-2034 employment outlook. The packet discloses that these occupation-level facts are broader than six-digit residential fee management.

The NAA/AppFolio article reports a survey of more than 2,000 professionals, AI use rising from 21 percent in 2024 to 34 percent in 2025, future-use plans, fee-manager revenue actions, and cyber concerns. The packet correctly treats adoption as a vendor-sponsored proxy rather than realized labor substitution.

The Census second-quarter 2025 release reports a 7.0 percent national rental vacancy rate and 65.0 percent homeownership rate. The packet explicitly identifies these as broad housing-stock context rather than a direct measure of outsourced management demand.

Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, based on a fall 2024 cross-industry survey. The packet transparently discounts intentions to an industry-specific completed-transfer estimate and states the resulting limitations.

caveats: S1's live cited page did not expose the specific 531311 definition in fetched content. This is a citation-support gap, but it does not establish a numeric correction to e, which is already labeled ESTIMATE, and therefore does not meet the base-tier-or-action materiality test.

The a estimate maps a broad NAICS 531000 occupational mix and broad manager duties into a six-digit wage-weighted assistability share without a direct task study. The PROXY label, bridge, wide range, and caveats disclose that judgment.

The rho estimate converts vendor-sponsored reported AI use into five-year realized implementation, although adoption does not measure workflow coverage or labor substitution. This limitation is explicitly disclosed and the primitive remains PROXY.

The q and o values are bounded ESTIMATE judgments; the cited operational evidence establishes contract, fee-manager, service, and physical-accountability context but does not directly measure post-AI value retention or technology-driven insourcing.

The d5 bridge combines a broad occupation employment projection with point-in-time national housing indicators rather than direct third-party-managed unit or constant-price fee-volume data; the packet discloses the population and construct mismatch.
The s5 bridge starts from cross-industry owner intentions rather than completed qualifying control transfers. The discount, PROXY state, and range make the limitation explicit.
summary: Mechanics pass and all six primitive-cited sources were reopened. The record is useful and internally coherent under the disclosed residential fee-management lens, with no reversed semantics, hidden proxy, double counting, or range defect that meets the frozen materiality test. Its PRIORITY tier and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action appropriately sit on estimate- and proxy-heavy evidence. Publication should retain the citation gap for S1 and the disclosed limitations of the broad occupational, adoption, succession, demand, retention, and substitution bridges.
